key_insight: Rough, deformable lubricated contacts can exhibit plug flow and cavitation, and a two-fluid lubricant outperforms single-component water or dodecane by lowering friction and material transfer while delaying flow breakdown.
engineering_relevance: The results suggest that mixed-lubricant strategies may improve boundary and mixed-lubrication performance in real rough contacts without relying on polymer brushes.
method: Non-equilibrium molecular dynamics of sliding between rough, deformable surfaces
Compared water, n-dodecane, and an immiscible two-fluid lubricant under identical geometry
Tracked friction, load-bearing behavior, plug flow, cavitation, and wear response
evidence_signal: The simulations show water has stronger speed dependence but lower load-bearing capacity than n-dodecane, while the two-fluid system has the lowest friction and material transfer and maintains plug flow to the lowest sliding speed.
watch_item: The findings come from atomistic simulations, so real-contact performance may depend on surface chemistry, scale, and lubricant miscibility beyond the modeled conditions.
paper_title: Plug Flow and Cavitation in Rough Lubricated Contacts: Molecular Dynamics of Single- vs. Two-Component Fluids
